musicalLanguage.description: Bethena is Scott Joplin's 1905 concert waltz, subtitled "A Concert Waltz" — a slower, harmonically ambitious cousin to his 2/4 rags, and the piece critics point to when arguing Joplin wanted ragtime taken seriously as art music rather than novelty. The 219-measure form (3/4 time, tempo marking equivalent to roughly 50 measures per minute felt in one) moves through several distinct waltz strains rather than settling on a single tonal center: the harness's own key-detection pass finds no single dominant tonic across the whole piece — the best whole-song match is G major at only a 0.124 correlation margin, and the stated Bb major correlates just as modestly at 0.347 — because Bethena genuinely modulates strain to strain the way a real concert waltz suite does. The opening waltz idea, first stated at measures 5-27, is substantial enough that Joplin brings it back twice more, note for note, at measures 68-88 and again at 195-219 — a structural refrain that ties an otherwise wandering set of strains back to one memorable idea.
musicalLanguage.structure: A four-bar introduction (measures 1-4) opens the piece, its own internal figure restated near-identically bar to bar. The main waltz strain (measures 5-27) establishes G major/E minor territory (Am7-D7-G-A7-Em chord windows) before a modulatory sequence (measures 28-61) settles into the stated Bb major (A#-D#-Gm7-F7 windows match the config key here). A chromatic bridge (measures 82-97) passes through E7-G#m-C#7-Fm before landing in F/C major (measures 93-121). A further shift to B minor/D major (measures 127-190) precedes the final return of the opening strain (measures 195-219), which restates measures 5-27 in full.
keyMoments: Measure 67, the sparsest bar in the piece (a single onset), is a written breath immediately before the opening strain returns note for note at measure 68 through measure 88. Hearing that silence as preparation, not emptiness, is what this passage teaches.

Measures 28-45 are the piece's busiest stretch (10-11 onsets in measures 29, 31, 37, and 39 alone) — the chord windows here read A#-D#-Gm7-F7, the one passage where the stated Bb major key signature is actually confirmed by the content, not just asserted.

Measures 82-97 move through E7-G#m-C#7-Fm, four chords in a row with no two sharing a diatonic key — Bethena's most harmonically restless passage, and recognizing it as a deliberate modulatory bridge rather than a mistake is the reward for following the harmony window by window.

The final return at measures 195-219 closes the piece by restating its own opening: the harness's pattern pass independently tracks a 46-interval left-hand figure (evidence grade: strong) recurring at measures 8-24, 67-83, and 195-211 — Joplin gives Bethena a genuine recapitulation, unusual for a rag-adjacent form.
teachingGoals: Practice each waltz strain as its own harmonic island before trying to connect them — measures 5-27, 28-61, 82-97, and 127-190 each live in different key areas, and treating the whole piece as one key will misread the harmony.

Learn to play a true one-beat-per-bar waltz pulse at this tempo (roughly 50 measures per minute) rather than counting three separate beats — the left hand's job is to make the barline, not the individual beat, the unit of motion.

Recognize a chromatic mediant bridge by ear: measures 82-97 move root to root in thirds rather than the fourths and fifths of a normal cadence. Identify where a passage stops behaving like a cadence and starts behaving like a sequence.

Build the stamina to hold a 219-measure form in memory by leaning on the recap — once you know measures 5-27 return whole at 68-88 and 195-219, you have effectively memorized three sections by learning one.
styleTips: Listen for the left hand disappearing almost entirely at measure 67 — that one-onset bar is a written silence, not a mistake in the score. Let it actually go quiet before the recap re-enters at measure 68.
Play the modulatory bridge at measures 82-97 with a slight give in tempo — a hair of rubato on each new chord lets the ear register the key shift instead of blurring past it.
Feel the waltz pulse in one, not three: listen for whether your downbeat lands like a dancer's single decisive step per bar rather than three equal ticks.

Voice the left-hand waltz bass so the low root (as deep as D#1 at measure 218) actually rings under the chord above it — a thin, undersupported bass makes the harmonic modulations impossible to follow by ear.
